schools.summary: Gill: after the LORD had given Moses instructions about building the tabernacle, the model of which he had shown him, he now speaks of the men to make it; the witnesses note God speaking again to Moses, to name the workmen who should make the tabernacle and its furniture — the turning from the pattern to the workmen, that God provides the men as well as the plan (the LORD now appointing who shall do the work: 'the LORD spake unto Moses, saying'; the connexion, that this followed after the LORD had given Moses instructions about building the tabernacle and all its furniture; that, having shown the pattern, God now provides for the doing of it, naming and fitting the men; the order, that God appoints both the work and the workmen, the plan and the hands to execute it; the comfort the commentators draw, that God who calls to a work also fits and provides for it, giving the men and the means; that nothing is wanting where God appoints, for he supplies all; that the same God who designs his house raises up those who shall build it).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses
saying
the LORD turning from the pattern of the tabernacle to the workmen who should make it
God who calls to a work also fits and provides for it, raising up those who shall build his house

schools.summary: Gill: God had pitched upon, chosen, and distinguished Bezaleel particularly from all other men to be the architect or master-builder of the tabernacle; the witnesses note God declaring he had called Bezaleel by name, the son of Uri, of the tribe of Judah, to be the chief workman — the divine choosing and naming of the master-builder (God himself singling out the man for the work: 'See, I have called by name Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah'; the calling, 'I have called by name Bezaleel,' that God had pitched upon, chosen, and distinguished him particularly from all other men, calling him by name to the work; the man, 'the son of Uri, the son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah,' of an honourable line, Hur being he who held up Moses' hands, and of the royal tribe of Judah; the dignity, that to be called by name of God to a service is a high honour, God himself appointing the man; the meaning the commentators draw, that God calls his servants by name to their work, as he calls his people, knowing and choosing each particularly; that the chief workman of God's house was of Judah, the tribe of Christ; that God fits and names whom he will for his service).

themes: See, I have called by name Bezaleel the son of Uri, the son of Hur
of the tribe of Judah
God calling Bezaleel by name, singling him out to be the master-builder of the tabernacle
God calling his servants by name to their work, knowing and choosing each particularly

schools.summary: Gill: he was filled, not with the special graces of the Spirit, or with spiritual gifts fitting for spiritual service, but with skill, wisdom, and ability for this work; the witnesses note God declaring he had filled Bezaleel with the Spirit of God, in wisdom, understanding, knowledge, and all workmanship — the gift of the Spirit for skill and ability in the work (God's Spirit fitting men even for the work of the hands: 'I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge, and in all manner of workmanship'; the filling, 'filled him with the spirit of God,' that this was not the special graces of the Spirit, or spiritual gifts fitting for spiritual service, but the Spirit giving skill and ability for the work; the gifts named, 'wisdom... understanding... knowledge... all manner of workmanship,' wisdom to design, understanding to order, knowledge to execute, and skill in every kind of craft; the great lesson the commentators draw, that all ability and skill, even in arts and crafts, is the gift of God's Spirit, who fits men for whatever work he calls them to; that the wisdom of the workman, as of the believer, comes from above; that God qualifies whom he calls, filling them for the work).

debates.question: What does Bezaleel's being 'filled with the spirit of God' for the work teach?

sides.position: That all ability and skill, even in arts and crafts, is the gift of God's Spirit — here not the special graces of the Spirit but skill and ability for the work — who fits and qualifies men for whatever work he calls them to

themes: I have filled him with the spirit of God, in wisdom, and in understanding, and in knowledge
and in all manner of workmanship
Bezaleel filled with the Spirit of God for skill and ability in the work
all ability and skill, even in arts and crafts, the gift of God's Spirit, who fits men for the work he calls them to

schools.summary: Gill: he was enabled to invent, contrive, and draw patterns for the weavers, and for making the curtains and other parts, and to work in the precious metals; the witnesses note God fitting Bezaleel to devise cunning works and to work in gold, silver, and brass — the skill given to design and to work the metals (the Spirit giving him art to invent and to fashion: 'To devise cunning works, to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass'; the devising, 'to devise cunning works,' that he was able to invent, contrive, and draw patterns, to design the curious work; the metals, 'to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass,' that he had skill to fashion the three metals of the tabernacle, the gold of the holy things, the silver of the foundation, and the brass of the court; that this skill, both to design and to execute, was the gift of the Spirit; the meaning, that God gives both the inventing mind and the working hand, the device and the doing; that all the curious and beautiful work of God's house was wrought by Spirit-given skill; that the arts that serve God's worship are his own gift).

themes: To devise cunning works
to work in gold, and in silver, and in brass
Bezaleel fitted to devise cunning works and to work the three metals
both the inventing mind and the working hand the gift of the Spirit, the arts of God's worship his own gift

schools.summary: Gill: the stones were not common building stones, but precious stones to be cut and set, as those of the ephod and breastplate, and he was skilled also in carving wood; the witnesses note God fitting Bezaleel in cutting and setting stones and carving timber, in all manner of workmanship — the further skill in the precious stones and the wood (his Spirit-given art reaching every craft of the work: 'in cutting of stones, to set them, and in carving of timber, to work in all manner of workmanship'; the stones, 'cutting of stones, to set them,' not common stones, but precious stones to be cut and set, as the gems of the ephod and breastplate; the timber, 'carving of timber,' skill to carve the shittim wood of the boards and other parts; the breadth, 'to work in all manner of workmanship,' that his skill reached every kind of craft needed for the tabernacle, metal, stone, and wood; the meaning, that the Spirit fitted him for the whole work, in all its variety, leaving nothing beyond his given skill; that God supplies all the gifts needed for his service, none lacking; that the whole work of the house was provided for in the one Spirit-filled workman and his fellows).

themes: And in cutting of stones, to set them, and in carving of timber
to work in all manner of workmanship
Bezaleel skilled in cutting and setting precious stones and carving timber, in all craft
the Spirit fitting him for the whole work, God supplying all the gifts needed for his service, none lacking

schools.summary: Gill: Aholiab, of the tribe of Dan, was given to be a partner with Bezaleel and to assist in the work, and wisdom was put into the hearts of all the wise-hearted; the witnesses note God giving Aholiab to help Bezaleel, and putting wisdom in the hearts of all the wise-hearted, that they might make all he commanded — the appointing of a helper and the gifting of many for the work (God providing fellow-workers and filling them all with skill: 'I have given with him Aholiab... and in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom, that they may make all that I have commanded thee'; the helper, 'I have given with him Aholiab... of the tribe of Dan,' a partner and assistant to Bezaleel, of another tribe, that the work might be shared; the many, 'in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom,' that God put skill into the hearts of all the able workmen, not Bezaleel and Aholiab only; the end, 'that they may make all that I have commanded thee,' that all this skill was given for the doing of God's commanded work, after the pattern; the meaning the commentators draw, that God gives variety of gifts and many workers for his work, each fitted in his place, and that even the wisdom of the hands is his putting; that the whole work is carried by many, all gifted of God).

themes: I have given with him Aholiab, the son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan
and in the hearts of all that are wise hearted I have put wisdom, that they may make all that I have commanded thee
Aholiab given as helper and wisdom put in all the wise-hearted, that they might make all God commanded
God gives variety of gifts and many workers for his work, each fitted in his place, the wisdom of the hands his putting

schools.summary: Gill: they had wisdom given to make the tabernacle according to the pattern showed to Moses, and under the direction of Bezaleel and Aholiab, to whom Moses communicated it; the witnesses note God naming the tabernacle, the ark of the testimony, the mercy seat, and all the furniture, among the things to be made — the beginning of the list of holy things the gifted workmen should make (all the holy things to be wrought after the pattern: 'The tabernacle of the congregation, and the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon, and all the furniture'; the order, that from henceforward mention is made of all the things to be made by the Spirit-filled workmen, beginning with the chief; the tabernacle itself, 'the tabernacle of the congregation,' God's dwelling; the ark and mercy seat, 'the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon,' the holiest things, where God met his people; that all was to be made according to the pattern showed to Moses, nothing of man's devising; the meaning, that the holy things, each with its meaning and type of Christ, were now to be wrought by the gifted men; that God's house and all its holy things are made by his own appointment and gifting).

themes: The tabernacle of the congregation, and the ark of the testimony, and the mercy seat that is thereupon
and all the furniture of the tabernacle
the tabernacle, ark, and mercy seat named among the things the gifted workmen should make
all wrought after the pattern, nothing of man's devising, God's house made by his own appointment and gifting

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the pure candlestick was so called either from the pure gold of which it was made, or the brightness and splendour of its workmanship, or the light which it gave; the witnesses note God naming the table with its furniture, the pure candlestick with its furniture, and the altar of incense, to be made — the furniture of the holy place to be wrought by the gifted men (the table, light, and incense-altar to be made: 'the table and his furniture, and the pure candlestick with all his furniture, and the altar of incense'; the table, 'the table and his furniture,' the shewbread table, with its dishes, spoons, and bowls; the candlestick, 'the pure candlestick with all his furniture,' so called from the pure gold of which it was made, or the brightness of its workmanship, or the light which it gave, with its lamps and snuffers; the incense-altar, 'the altar of incense,' the golden altar where the sweet incense was burned; that these three, the table, the candlestick, and the altar of incense, were the furniture of the holy place; the meaning the commentators draw, that the shewbread, Christ the bread of life, the candlestick, Christ the light, and the incense-altar, the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession, were each a type; that all the holy furniture was to be wrought by the gifted men).

themes: And the table and his furniture, and the pure candlestick with all his furniture
and the altar of incense
the table, candlestick, and altar of incense named among the furniture of the holy place
each a type: the shewbread (Christ the bread of life), the candlestick (Christ the light), the incense-altar (the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession)

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the altar of burnt offering, and the laver and its foot, the pedestal on which it stood; the witnesses note God naming the altar of burnt offering with its furniture, and the laver and its foot, to be made — the vessels of the court to be wrought by the gifted men (the altar and laver to be made: 'the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture, and the laver and his foot'; the altar, 'the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture,' the brazen altar, made of shittim wood covered with brass, with its pans, shovels, basins, and the rest of its vessels; the laver, 'the laver and his foot,' the brazen laver, with the pedestal on which it stood, where the priests washed; that these were the vessels of the court, the altar of sacrifice and the laver of washing, the first things met on entering; the meaning the commentators draw, that the brazen altar, the cross of Christ where sacrifice is made, and the laver, the washing of regeneration and the word, were each a type, the way to God being through sacrifice and cleansing; that all the vessels, of court and sanctuary, were to be made by the gifted men).

themes: And the altar of burnt offering with all his furniture
and the laver and his foot
the brazen altar of burnt offering and the laver named among the vessels of the court
each a type: the brazen altar (the cross of Christ) and the laver (the washing of regeneration), the way to God through sacrifice and cleansing

schools.summary: Gill: the cloths of service were either those the priests ministered in during the time of service, and never wore but when in it, or the cloths of blue and purple in which the holy things were wrapped; the witnesses note God naming the cloths of service and the holy garments of Aaron and his sons, to minister in the priest's office, to be made — the priestly vestments and service-cloths to be wrought by the gifted men (the garments of the priests to be made: 'the cloths of service, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, to minister in the priest's office'; the cloths of service, that these were either those the priests ministered in only in the time of service, or cloths of blue and purple in which the holy vessels were wrapped for carriage; the holy garments, 'the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons,' the high priest's glorious vestments and the plainer garments of the common priests, all to minister in the priest's office; that the garments too, like the furniture, were to be wrought by the gifted men; the type, that the holy garments shadow the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service; that all that pertains to God's service, even the garments, is made by his appointment).

themes: And the cloths of service, and the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons
to minister in the priest's office
the cloths of service and the holy garments of the priests named among the things to be made
a type of the righteousness and graces with which Christ and his people are clothed for God's service

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the anointing oil and the sweet incense, of the composition of both which directions had been given; the witnesses note God naming the anointing oil and the sweet incense for the holy place, to be made, with the charge that all be done according to all he had commanded — the last of the holy things and the seal of the whole, that all be made after the pattern (the oil and incense completing the list, and all by command: 'the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place: according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do'; the oil and incense, 'the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place,' the holy oil of consecration and the sweet incense, each of a special composition appointed by God; the type, that the anointing oil shadows the Spirit and the sweet incense the prayers of the saints and Christ's intercession, a sweet savour before God; the great charge, 'according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do,' that all, furniture and garments and oil and incense, was to be made exactly according to all God commanded, nothing of man's devising; the meaning the commentators draw, that in God's service all is to be done by his rule and command, not by man's invention; that God will be served only his own way).

debates.question: What does 'according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do' teach?

sides.position: That in God's service all is to be done by his rule and command — furniture, garments, oil, and incense all made exactly as God commanded, nothing of man's devising, for God will be served only his own way

themes: And the anointing oil, and sweet incense for the holy place
according to all that I have commanded thee shall they do
the anointing oil and sweet incense named last, all to be made according to all God commanded
the oil a type of the Spirit, the incense of prayer and Christ's intercession; in God's service all by his rule, not man's invention

schools.summary: Gill: after describing the place of worship, appointing the priests, and ordering all things and the workmen, God repeats the law of the sabbath and reminds of the time of worship; the witnesses note God speaking again to Moses, now to repeat the sabbath law after all the orders about the tabernacle — the turning from the place and work of worship to the sabbath, the time of worship (having ordered the tabernacle, God now guards the sabbath: 'the LORD spake unto Moses, saying'; the connexion, that this came after he had described the place of worship, appointed the priests, ordered the making of all things, and the workmen; that now God repeats the law of the sabbath, and puts in mind of the time of worship, having ordered the place; the great meaning the commentators draw, that even the holy work of building the tabernacle must not break the sabbath, the time of rest being as sacred as the place of worship; that God will have his sabbath kept even amid his own work; that the order of place and time in worship is of God's appointing).

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses
saying
the LORD speaking again to repeat the sabbath law after all the orders about the tabernacle
even the holy work of building the tabernacle must not break the sabbath, the time of rest as sacred as the place of worship

schools.summary: Gill: notwithstanding all that was ordered about making the tabernacle, this was not to be understood to the violation of the sabbath, in which no work was to be done, not even any relating to the tabernacle; the witnesses note God charging Israel to keep his sabbaths, for it was a sign between him and them throughout their generations, that they might know he is the LORD who sanctifies them — the sabbath as a sign of God's sanctifying his people (the sabbath marking them as the LORD's, whom he sanctifies: 'Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it is a sign between me and you... that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you'; the charge, 'my sabbaths ye shall keep,' that, even amid the work of the tabernacle, no work was to be done, not even any relating to the tabernacle, on the sabbath; the sign, 'it is a sign between me and you throughout your generations,' that the sabbath was a token of the covenant between God and Israel; the great end, 'that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you,' that the keeping of the sabbath, set apart to God, witnessed that he had set apart and sanctified them as his own; the meaning, that as God sanctified the day, so he sanctifies his people, the rest a sign of the holiness he works in them; the type, that the sabbath shadows the rest and sanctification God's people have in Christ).

debates.question: What does the sabbath, kept as a sign, witness?

sides.position: That the LORD sanctifies his people — the sabbath, set apart to God, a sign that he had set apart and sanctified Israel as his own; as God sanctified the day, so he sanctifies his people, the rest a sign of the holiness he works in them, shadowing the rest in Christ

themes: Verily my sabbaths ye shall keep: for it is a sign between me and you throughout your generations
that ye may know that I am the LORD that doth sanctify you
the sabbath a sign between God and Israel that the LORD sanctifies them
as God sanctified the day, so he sanctifies his people, the rest shadowing the rest his people have in Christ

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: to defile the sabbath is by any kind of idolatrous or profane worship, and the magistrates were to examine the business and put the guilty to death; the witnesses note God charging Israel to keep the sabbath, for it was holy, with death the penalty for defiling it or working on it — the strictness of the sabbath law and its solemn sanction (the sabbath kept holy on pain of death: 'Ye shall keep the sabbath therefore; for it is holy unto you: every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death'; the charge, 'keep the sabbath... for it is holy unto you,' strictly to observe it, according to the rules given, as a holy day; the sanction, 'every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death,' that to defile the sabbath by any kind of idolatrous or profane act, or to do work in it, was a capital crime, the magistrates to try and execute the guilty; the cutting off, 'that soul shall be cut off from among his people,' destroyed and rooted out from Israel; the meaning the commentators draw, that the solemn severity marks how high God values the keeping of his holy day and his worship; that the breach of God's holy things is no light matter; that holiness is to be kept on pain of his judgment).

themes: Ye shall keep the sabbath therefore; for it is holy unto you: every one that defileth it shall surely be put to death
for whosoever doeth any work therein, that soul shall be cut off from among his people
the sabbath to be kept holy on pain of death for defiling it or working on it
the solemn severity marking how high God values his holy day, the breach of his holy things no light matter

schools.summary: Gill: six days work may be done, allowed an Israelite if he would, this being not a command to work but a permission; and seeing they had so many days, it was not unreasonable that God should claim one in seven; the witnesses note God allowing work the six days but appointing the seventh the sabbath of rest, holy to him, on pain of death for working in it — the granting of the six days and the claiming of the seventh (God giving six and claiming one: 'Six days may work be done; but in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD'; the grant, 'Six days may work be done,' that this is not a command to work, but a permission, the six days allowed if he would; the reasonableness, that, seeing they had so many days granted them, it could not be thought hard that God should claim one day in seven for his own; the seventh, 'in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD,' a day of rest, set apart and holy to God; the sanction, 'whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day... shall surely be put to death,' the same solemn penalty repeated; the meaning, that God deals bountifully, giving six and asking but one, and that one for our rest and his worship; that it is just and reasonable to give God his day).

themes: Six days may work be done; but in the seventh is the sabbath of rest, holy to the LORD
whosoever doeth any work in the sabbath day, he shall surely be put to death
God allowing work the six days (a permission) but claiming the seventh as the holy sabbath of rest
God dealing bountifully, giving six days and asking but one, and that one for our rest and his worship

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: it is called a perpetual covenant because it is a sign of that future rest and blessedness which remains for the people of God; Gill marks the seventh-day sabbath as ceremonial and shadowy; the witnesses note God appointing Israel to keep the sabbath through all their generations as a perpetual covenant — the lasting obligation of the sabbath and its covenant meaning (the sabbath a standing covenant-sign: 'the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath, to observe the sabbath throughout their generations, for a perpetual covenant'; the duty, 'shall keep the sabbath... throughout their generations,' that the sabbath was binding on Israel in every age; the covenant, 'for a perpetual covenant,' that it was a lasting sign of the covenant between God and his people; the meaning Clarke draws, that it is a perpetual covenant because it is a sign of that future rest and blessedness which remains for the people of God; that Gill marks the seventh-day sabbath as ceremonial and shadowy, a shadow of the rest to come, the substance being Christ; that the sabbath points beyond itself to the everlasting rest in God; that God's people are bound to honour his rest, which shadows the rest he gives).

debates.question: Why is the sabbath called a 'perpetual covenant'?

sides.position: Because it is a sign of that future rest and blessedness which remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9) — the seventh-day sabbath ceremonial and shadowy, pointing beyond itself to the everlasting rest in God, the substance being Christ

themes: Wherefore the children of Israel shall keep the sabbath, to observe the sabbath throughout their generations
for a perpetual covenant
Israel bound to keep the sabbath through all generations as a perpetual covenant-sign
a sign of the future rest that remains for the people of God, the seventh-day sabbath shadowy, the substance Christ

schools.summary: Gill: it is a sign for ever in like sense as the land of Canaan was given for an everlasting possession, and circumcision and the passover were for ever, that is, unto the end of the Jewish state; the witnesses note God declaring the sabbath a sign between him and Israel for ever, grounded in his making heaven and earth in six days and resting the seventh — the perpetual sign and its ground in creation (the sabbath resting on God's own resting: 'It is a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed'; the sign, 'a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever,' for ever in the sense that the land, circumcision, and the passover were for ever, that is, unto the end of the Jewish state, the ceremonial sabbath lasting till Christ; the ground, 'in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, and on the seventh day he rested,' that the weekly rest was founded on God's own resting after the six days of creation; the bold word, 'and was refreshed,' spoken after the manner of men, that God, as it were, took complacency and delight in his finished work, resting in it; the meaning, that man's rest follows God's, and that the sabbath roots in creation itself; the type, that God's rest, and the sabbath, point to the rest that remains for his people in Christ).

themes: It is a sign between me and the children of Israel for ever: for in six days the LORD made heaven and earth
and on the seventh day he rested, and was refreshed
the sabbath a sign grounded in God's making heaven and earth in six days and resting the seventh
'was refreshed' spoken after the manner of men; man's rest following God's, pointing to the rest that remains in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: after all those laws and instructions, God ceased to converse with Moses in that manner, and at parting gave him two tables of testimony, the two tables of the law; the witnesses note God giving Moses, when their communing ended, the two tables of testimony, tables of stone written with his own finger — the close of the communing and the gift of the law in stone (God sealing all by giving the two tables written by himself: 'he gave unto Moses, when he had made an end of communing with him upon mount Sinai, two tables of testimony, tables of stone, written with the finger of God'; the close, 'when he had made an end of communing with him,' that, after all those laws, orders, and instructions, God ceased to converse with him in that manner; the gift, 'two tables of testimony, tables of stone,' the two tables of the law, the Ten Commandments, called the testimony, graven on stone; the author, 'written with the finger of God,' that the law was written not by man but with the finger of God, by his own immediate hand and power; the meaning the commentators draw, that the moral law, being God's own writing, is of his own authority, abiding and sure, the rule of his people; that the law on stone shadows the law to be written by the Spirit on the heart; that God himself is the author of his law).

debates.question: What does the law being 'written with the finger of God' signify?

sides.position: That the moral law is of God's own authority — written not by man but by God's own immediate hand and power, abiding and sure, the rule of his people; the law on stone shadowing the law to be written by the Spirit on the heart (2 Corinthians 3:3; Jeremiah 31:33)

themes: he gave unto Moses, when he had made an end of communing with him upon mount Sinai, two tables of testimony, tables of stone
written with the finger of God
the two tables of testimony, the law graven on stone, written with God's own finger
the moral law of God's own authority, the law on stone shadowing the law written by the Spirit on the heart
